content:
# Jude Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2JN_001_page_001.jpg, JUD_001_page_001.jpg

1. Jude, a servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, to those who are called, beloved in God the Father and kept for Jesus Christ:

2. May mercy, peace, and love be multiplied to you.

3. Beloved, although I was very eager to write to you about our common salvation, I found it necessary to write appealing to you to contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.

4. For certain people have crept in unnoticed who long ago were designated for this condemnation, ungodly people, who pervert the grace of our God into sensuality and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.

5. Now I want to remind you, although you once fully knew it, that the Lord, who saved a people out of the land of Egypt, afterward destroyed those who did not believe.

6. And the angels who did not stay within their own position of authority, but left their proper dwelling, he has kept in eternal chains under gloomy darkness until the judgment of the great day —

7. just as Sodom and Gomorrah and the surrounding cities, which likewise indulged in sexual immorality and pursued unnatural desire, serve as an example by undergoing a punishment of eternal fire.

8. Yet in like manner these people also, relying on their dreams, defile the flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme the glorious ones.

9. But when the archangel Michael, contending with the devil, was disputing about the body of Moses, he did not presume to pronounce a blasphemous judgment, but said, "The Lord rebuke you."

10. But these people blaspheme all that they do not understand, and they are destroyed by all that they, like unreasoning animals, understand instinctively.

11. Woe to them! For they walked in the way of Cain and abandoned themselves for the sake of gain to Balaam's error and perished in Korah's rebellion.

12. These are hidden reefs at your love feasts, as they feast with you without fear, shepherds feeding themselves; waterless clouds, swept along by winds; fruitless trees in late autumn, twice dead, uprooted;

13. wild waves of the sea, casting up the foam of their own shame; wandering stars, for whom the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved forever.

14. It was also about these that Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied, saying, "Behold, the Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones,

15. to execute judgment on all and to convict all the ungodly of all their deeds of ungodliness that they have committed in such an ungodly way, and of all the harsh things that ungodly sinners have spoken against him."

16. These are grumblers, malcontents, following their own sinful desires; they are loud-mouthed boasters, showing favoritism to gain advantage.

17. But you must remember, beloved, the predictions of the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ.

18. They said to you, "In the last time there will be scoffers, following their own ungodly passions."

19. It is these who cause divisions, worldly people, devoid of the Spirit.

20. But you, beloved, building yourselves up in your most holy faith and praying in the Holy Spirit,

21. keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that leads to eternal life.

22. And have mercy on those who doubt;

23. save others by snatching them out of the fire; to others show mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh.

24. Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy,

25. to the only God, our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever. Amen.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Jude is a brief, fiery letter defending "the faith once for all delivered to the saints" (v.3) against false teachers who have infiltrated the community. The letter is notable for its use of extra-canonical sources: the Assumption of Moses (v.9) and 1 Enoch (vv.14-15). The four nature metaphors (v.12-13) are among the most vivid in the NT. The doxology (vv.24-25) is one of the most beloved in Christian liturgy.

**Verse 3 — "Once for all delivered":** hapax paradotheisē — the faith is not an evolving tradition but a deposit, given definitively. This verse is the letter's thesis statement.

**Verses 22-23 — The three responses:** Mercy to doubters, rescue for those in danger, cautious mercy for the deeply compromised. The textual tradition here is complex — Sinaiticus preserves a three-part structure that some manuscripts reduce to two.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "a servant of Jesus Christ" vs. "a slave of Jesus Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a servant of Yeshua Christos" | Ioudas Iēsou Christou doulos |
| LOGOS | "a servant of Jesus Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a slave of Jesus Christ" | "Slave" for doulos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. doulos means "slave/servant" — both are valid translations. "Servant" is the traditional English rendering that preserves the honor of the title (being God's servant was a title of distinction in Israel — cf. Moses, David). "Slave" is more etymologically precise but carries modern connotations of degradation that the ancient honor-title did not. In a letter where the author introduces himself with this word as a credential, "servant" better captures the intended dignity.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Both are valid; "servant" preserves the honor-connotation.

---

### v.4 — "sensuality" vs. "live immoral lives"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "perverting the grace of our God into licentiousness" | tēn tou theou hēmōn charita metatithentes eis aselgeian |
| LOGOS | "who pervert the grace of our God into sensuality" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "saying that God's marvelous grace allows us to live immoral lives" | Adds "saying that... allows us to"; "marvelous" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says they "pervert" (metatithentes — "transfer/change") grace into aselgeia ("sensuality/licentiousness"). PNEUMA restructures this into a doctrinal claim: "saying that God's marvelous grace allows us to live immoral lives." While this is the practical implication, the author describes an action (perverting grace), not a teaching (saying grace allows immorality). PNEUMA also adds "marvelous," which is not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA converts action into doctrine.

---

### v.12 — "hidden reefs at your love feasts" vs. "dangerous reefs that can shipwreck you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] in your love feasts, feasting together without fear" | houtoi eisin hoi en tais agapais hymōn spilades syneuōchoumenoi aphobōs |
| LOGOS | "hidden reefs at your love feasts, as they feast with you without fear" | spilades = reefs/rocks |
| PNEUMA | "dangerous reefs that can shipwreck you" | Adds "shipwreck"; drops "love feasts" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. spilades means "reefs" or "rocks" — hidden dangers at the communal meal. PNEUMA adds "that can shipwreck you" (not in the text) and drops the crucial phrase "at your love feasts" (en tais agapais hymōn). The love feast (agapē meal) is the specific setting — these false teachers are present at the community's sacred meals. Dropping this detail loses the social specificity that makes the metaphor so alarming.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA drops "love feasts" and adds "shipwreck."

---

### v.24 — "to present you blameless before the presence of his glory" vs. "will bring you with great joy into his glorious presence without a single fault"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to set [you] before his glory blameless with great joy" | stēsai katenōpion tēs doxēs autou amōmous en agalliasei |
| LOGOS | "to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "will bring you with great joy into his glorious presence without a single fault" | "Glorious presence" for "presence of his glory"; "without a single fault" for "blameless" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says "before his glory" (katenōpion tēs doxēs autou) — glory is a noun, not an adjective. PNEUMA's "glorious presence" makes glory modify presence, but the author says you will stand before glory itself — the shekinah, the kabod, the radiant presence. "Without a single fault" for amōmous ("blameless/unblemished") is adequate but less resonant — amōmos is sacrificial language (an unblemished offering) that connects to the letter's concern with purity.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA converts noun to adjective.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA converts action to doctrine | 1 | "Saying grace allows" for "perverting grace into" (v.4) |
| PNEUMA drops specific details | 1 | "Love feasts" omitted (v.12) |
| PNEUMA adds narrative elements | 1 | "Shipwreck" added (v.12) |
| PNEUMA converts noun to adjective | 1 | "Glorious presence" for "presence of his glory" (v.24) |

**Overall pattern:** Jude's disagreements center on PNEUMA's tendency to explain rather than preserve. The most significant is v.12 — dropping "love feasts" removes the specific social setting that makes the false teachers' presence so dangerous. They are not just false teachers in general; they are eating at the sacred communal meal. The doxology (v.24) shows the recurring pattern of converting nouns to adjectives: "his glory" becomes "his glorious" — losing the weight of glory as a thing you stand before.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
